steps.title: Analyze the structure after the comma
steps.content: The main independent clause ends with "lent Fairuz a timeless, cross-generational appeal." After the comma, what follows — "[blank] her the moniker 'the soul of Lebanon'" — must attach to that clause as a modifying phrase, not form a second independent clause.

steps.title: Test each form against the participial phrase rule
steps.content:
After a comma following an independent clause, a present participle introduces a modifying phrase showing result or circumstance. A finite verb form instead creates a comma splice (two independent clauses joined only by a comma).
  • Choice A — 'earned': Simple past finite verb — creates a comma splice by joining two independent clauses without a coordinating conjunction. ✗
  • Choice B — 'had earned': Past perfect finite verb — also creates a comma splice for the same reason. ✗
  • Choice C — 'earning': Present participle — introduces a participial phrase that correctly modifies the main clause by expressing the result of lending her that appeal. ✓
  • Choice D — 'earn': Base (infinitive) form — not a grammatical participial phrase and would require "to" to serve as an infinitive; does not conform to Standard English here. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
